items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils often nutrient-poor despite abundant vegetation?
items.correct: Rapid decomposition and uptake mean nutrients cycle quickly through living biomass, not soil.
items.misconception: Rainforests have rich soils because they are productive.
items.random_wrong: Rainforests are deserts most of the year.
items.why: Students assume productive = rich soil. Actually most nutrients in rainforests are in living biomass; rapid uptake leaves soils thin. That's why clear-cutting destroys long-term productivity quickly.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.correct: Benefits people obtain from ecosystems (pollination, water purification, climate regulation, food).
items.random_wrong: Services that ecosystems perform for themselves.
items.why: Students think 'services' means human services. The term refers to ecological functions that BENEFIT HUMANS - a framework for valuing nature in economic and policy decisions.

items.q: MacArthur and Wilson published island biogeography theory in:
items.why: It's 1967. “1962” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: IUCN Red List category 'EN' stands for:
items.why: Correct answer: Endangered. “Endemic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year the IUCN Red List was established?
items.why: The real figure is 1964 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
